source_metadata.title_raw: James R. Sutton - I-19-190a - March 30, 2020 - San Francisco

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916)
322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 30, 2020

Sutton Advice Letter No. I-19-190(a) is SUPERSEDED by Sutton Advice Letter No.
I-19-190

James R. Sutton
150 Post Street, Suite 405
San Francisco, CA 94108

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-19-190(a)

Dear Mr. Sutton:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes the facts are
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should
change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we
are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Your law firm represents numerous campaign committees that have reporting obligations
under the Act.

You seek advice as to whether certain types of payments made by campaign consulting
firms, law firms, and political treasurer firms retained by a committee on Schedule G of the committee's Form 460.³

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

Section 84211 of the Act specifies what information must be disclosed on campaign
statements. For expenditures of $100 or more made to a single payee, committees must provide the
name and address of the payee as well as the amount and a description of the payment. (Section

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

³ This letter supersedes the Sutton Advice Letter, No. I-19-190, and is issued in reconsideration of the issues
presented.

File No. 1-19-190
Page No. 2

84211(k).). For payments made through an independent contractor or agent, commonly referred to
as subvendor payments, Section 84211(k)(6) provides that committees must disclose this same
information for "each person, if different than the payee, who has provided consideration for an
expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more during the period covered by the campaign
statement." (Section 84211(k)(6).)

In conjunction with Section 84211(k)(6), Section 84303(a) provides that a committee must
report any expenditure of $500 or more made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the
committee as if the committee had made the expenditure itself, unless the expenditure is for
"overhead or normal operating expenses." Regulation 18431 also sets forth what types of
expenditures by a committee's agent or independent contractor must be reported under Section
84303, including payments to vendors and subvendors.⁴ Under Regulation 18431, three categories
of payments to a subvendor are reportable:

1) Analysis, advice, or management services in connection with:
• Development of campaign strategy
• Campaign management
• Design or management of campaign literature or advertising
• Campaign fundraising

Regulation 18431(a)(1).

2) Products or services that show how the campaign is conducted including but not limited to:
• Printed campaign literature
• Advertising time or space
• Campaign buttons and other campaign paraphernalia
• Surveys, polls, signature gathering, and door-to-door solicitation of voters
• Facilities, invitations or entertainment for fundraising events
• Postage for campaign mailings

Regulation 18431(a)(2).

3) Any of the following expenditures:
• To the candidate
• To any person for the personal use of the or his/her family
• Gifts to any person
• To or on behalf of any other candidate or committee
• Printers of mass mailings
• To any person who transfers funds to any other person for expenditures reportable under
Regulation 18431

_______________

⁴ Regulation 18431(c) also requires a committee to report its subvendor expenditures on its campaign
statements "at the same time and in the same manner and detail as required under Section 84211 for the […]
committee's direct expenditures."

File No. I-19-190
Page No. 3

• Any commission, finder's fee or rebate paid to a person in connection with any
reportable expenditure under Regulation 18431 that is inconsistent in either manner or
amount with customary industry practice

Regulation 18431(a)(3)

Thus, Regulation 18431 clearly details the types of payments to subvendors—including
specific products and services—which are reportable under Section 84303 and, thus, also required
to be itemized on a committee's campaign statements under Section 84211.

Regulation 18431(a) also provides that "expenditures not of the types included […above] do
not have to be reported." (Emphasis added.)

The plain text of Section 84303(a), exempts expenditures for "overhead or normal operating
expenses" from the subvendor reporting requirements. Therefore, read together with the provision
in Regulation 18431(a) that "expenditures not of the types included […above] do not have to be
reported," a committee is not required to report a subvendor payment if it is made for: i) "overhead
or normal operating expenses," which include the kinds of services that the committee's agent or
independent contractor is retained to provide; or ii) for a type of subvendor payment not mentioned
in Regulation 18431.

Interpreting these provisions, we have previously advised that Section 84303 did not require
disclosure of individual telephone bank workers or of the telephone company providing the rental
phones to the phone bank, because "these payments are normal operating expenses of a phone
bank." (Davidson Advice Letter, No. I-90-107.) More broadly, we advised in the Dayaloguer letter
that "Candidates and committees are not required to report expenditures made on their behalf by agents
or independent contractors if those expenditures are for other than [sic] the agent's or independent
contractor's normal operating and overhead expenses." Similarly, in the Trautwein Advice Letter,
No. I-87-315, we advised that Section 84303 did not require a candidate to report a campaign
button and bumper sticker manufacturer as a subvendor, so long as the candidate reported the
candidate's payments to the campaign management firm who ordered the paraphernalia on behalf of
the candidate, as well as the subvendor who supplied the campaign management firm with the
buttons and bumper stickers.

In light of recent informal assistance sought by email, you have made a number of inquiries
about different types of subvendor payments and whether they need to be itemized by a committee
in its campaign reports. We answer each one below.

1. Expenses incurred by a campaign consulting firm. Must the following payments made by a
political committee to a campaign consulting firm as reimbursement for expenses which the
consulting firm has incurred on behalf of the committee be disclosed as payments to subvendors
on Schedule G:

File No. 1-19-190
Page No. 4

A. Federal Express or other overnight delivery services to send materials to committee
representatives.

Regulation 18431(a)(2) provides that products or services related to postage for campaign
mailings must be reported as subvendor payments under Section 84303. However, internal
communications between a consulting firm and the committee would not generally be considered a
campaign mailing. Thus, Federal Express or other overnight delivery services for the
communications would be considered overhead or normal operating expenses and not reportable
subvendor payments under Section 84303.

B. Airlines and hotels for travel to meet with committee representatives or conduct other
business on behalf of the committee.

No. Travel and lodging costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm in connection with
regular services provided are not reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303 or
Regulation 18431. Travel and lodging do not constitute expert advice, analysis, or campaign
management services within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(1). Nor are travel and lodging
costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm for travel to conduct its services "products or services
that show how the campaign is conducted," within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(2). Thus,
airfare and hotel costs would be considered "overhead or normal operating" expenses under Section
84303 and not reportable subvendor payments.⁵

C. Hourly fees to canvassers, when the firm is retained to conduct grassroots outreach for the
committee.

"Canvassing" is generally understood to mean "to go through (a district) or go to (persons) in
order to solicit orders or political support or to determine opinions or sentiments."⁶ Regulation
18431(a)(2)(D) provides that subvendor payments for "surveys, polls, signature gathering and door-
to-door solicitation of voters" must be reported under Section 84303. (Emphasis added.) While the
committee must report payments for canvassing, payments to individual canvassers by a campaign
consulting firm hired by a committee may be reported as payments to the firm for the canvassing
services and not as separate subvendor payments to the individual canvassers. If reported in this
manner, names of individual canvassers need not be reported.⁷

⁵ We note that this conclusion does not apply if the campaign consulting firm is paying the travel and lodging
costs for a candidate or a member of his/her family.

⁶ "Canvass," Merriam-Webster Online Dictionary, 2019. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary (2 Dec.
2019).

⁷ While Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D), requires subvendor payments for "surveys, polls, signature gathering and
door-to-door solicitation of voters" to be reported in accordance with Sections 84211(k)(6) and 84303(e), we must read
Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D) in conjunction with applicable case law. The U.S. Supreme Court in Buckley v. American
Constitutional Law Foundation, 525 U.S. 182 (1999), struck down a Colorado law requiring identification of each paid
petition circulator and the total amount of money paid to each circulator. In light of Buckley, the Commission itself
supported emergency legislation to repeal former Section 84211(r) which had required similar disclosure. Accordingly,
while a subvendor must report any payment to a firm or business entity that is paid to provide signature gathering or
door-to-door solicitation of voters, the names of individual signature gatherers or door-to-door solicitors need not be
reported. By extension, the names of individual canvassers also need not be reported.

File No. 1-19-190
Page No. 5

D. Hourly fees to petition circulators, when the firm is retained by the committee to circulate an
initiative petition.

Similar to a payment for canvassers, payments to a campaign consulting firm for individual
petition circulators hired by the firm may be reported by the committee as payments to the firm for
its services and not as separate subvendor payments requiring the names of individual signature
gatherers.

(See I.C., above.)

E. Fees to another campaign consulting firm to help the firm develop strategic advice, when the
firm is retained to provide strategic advice to the committee.

Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) specifically provides that subvendor payments for advice
concerning campaign strategy must be reported.

F. Fees to outside graphic designers retained because the firm's in-house graphic designers are
at full capacity during the busy campaign season, when the firm is retained to create graphics,
logos, artwork, etc. for the committee.

Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor payments for products or services related to
campaign literature, campaign buttons and other paraphernalia, to be reported. Accordingly,
subvendor payments to on outside graphic designer for campaign materials must be reported.

2. Expenses and fees paid by a law firm. Do the following payments made by a political
committee to a law firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor payments?

A. Westlaw, Lexis, or other legal research services.

No. Legal research is "the kind of service" that a law firm retained to provide legal counsel
is hired to provide. These types of legal research services are customarily part of overhead or
normal operating expenses for a law firm. Such services need not be reported as subvendor
payments under Section 84303.

B. Filing fees to a court in connection with a lawsuit involving the committee.

No. Court filing fees are normal operating expenses and need not be reported
as subvendor payments under Section 84303.

C. Amounts paid to the state or local government to settle enforcement matters.

Yes. Payments to state and local governments for settlement amounts reached in
enforcement matters on behalf of clients must be reported by the committees directly and not as
payments by subvendors. These are not payments that fall within any exception from reporting.
They are not overhead or normal operating expenses of a subcontractor and they are not payments
for services. They are debts owed by a client that are passed through the law firm. If a law firm

File No. I-19-190
Page No. 6

sends payment to a government agency for a settlement on behalf of a client, the committee should
note this on its campaign reports when reporting the expenditure.

D. Expert witness fees in a lawsuit involving the committee.

No. See 2B above.

E. Fees paid to independent contractor attorneys to help the law firm research and prepare
pleadings for a lawsuit being pursued by the committee.

No. See 2A, above.8

3. Expenses and fees paid by a political treasurer firm. Do payments made by a political
committee to a political treasurer firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor
payments?

A. Banking fees.

No. Under the Act, it is a campaign treasurer's duty to ensure that the committee complies
with all requirements concerning receipts, expenditures, and reporting and recordkeeping.
(Sections 81004, 84100, 84213, and Regulation 18427). In carrying out these duties, a treasurer is
generally responsible for staying informed of bank deposits and proper expenditures, including
ensuring that the committee complies with the so-called "one bank account rule." (Section 85201
and Regulation 18521.)

Thus, a treasurer's duties often include management of the committee's bank account and it is
within the realm of the "kind of service" a political treasurer firm offers for the firm to cover a
committees banking fees.

B. Late filing fees paid to the Secretary of State or local filing officer.

Yes. See 2C, above.

C. Campaign reporting software licensing fees (e.g., Netfile, NGP, etc.).

No. These are normal operating expenses of campaign treasurers.

D. Fees to credit card processing firms for processing contributions (Democracy Engine,
ActBlue, etc.)

Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) provides that analysis, advice, or management services in
connection with, among other things, campaign fundraising must be reported as a subvendor
payment. Additionally, Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor reporting for products or
services that show how a campaign is conducted. While making payments to a credit card

_______________________
8 We note that this conclusion is limited to a law firm hired exclusively to research and prepare pleadings for a
lawsuit. Any other legal services provided may be reportable depending on the nature of the services.

File No. 1-19-190
Page No. 7

processing firm alone does not constitute analysis or advice, a campaign treasurer's payment of
a committee/candidate's ActBlue or Democracy Engine fees does constitute management
services in connection with campaign fundraising and also shows how a campaign is being
conducted. As such, these payments are reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303.

E. Contract data entry help during busy reporting seasons.

No. Data entry alone would fall under overhead or normal operating expenses that are kind
of services that the committee's agent or independent contractor is retained to provide.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Toren Lewis
Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:aja

sections.question:
S AND CONCLUSIONS 
Section 84211 of the Act specifies what information must be disclosed on campaign 
statements. For expenditures of $100 or more made to a single payee, committees must provide the 
name and address of the payee as well as the amount and a description of the payment. (Section2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
3 This letter supersedes the Sutton Advice Letter, No. I-19-190, and is issued in reconsideration of the issues 
presented.  
 
 
84211(k).) For payments made through an independent contractor or agent, commonly referred to 
as subvendor payments, Section 84211(k)(6) provides that committees must disclose this same 
information for “each person, if different than the payee, who has provided consideration for an 
expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more during the period covered by the campaign 
statement.” (Section 84211(k)(6).) 
 
In conjunction with Section 84211(k)(6), Section 84303(a) provides that a committee must 
report any expenditure of $500 or more made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the 
committee as if the committee had made the expenditure itself, unless the expenditure is for 
“overhead or normal operating expenses.” Regulation 18431 also sets forth what types of 
expenditures by a committee’s agent or independent contractor must be reported under Section 
84303, including payments to vendors and subvendors.4 Under Regulation 18431, three categories 
of payments to a subvendor are reportable: 
 
1) Analysis, advice, or management services in connection with: 
• Development of campaign strategy 
• Campaign management 
• Design or management of campaign literature or advertising 
• Campaign fundraising 
Regulation 18431(a)(1). 
2) Products or services that show how the campaign is conducted including but not limited to: 
• Printed campaign literature 
• Advertising time or space 
• Campaign buttons and other campaign paraphernalia 
• Surveys, polls, signature gathering, and door-to-door solicitation of voters 
• Facilities, invitations or entertainment for fundraising events 
• Postage for campaign mailings 
Regulation 18431(a)(2). 
3) Any of the following expenditures: 
• To the candidate 
• To any person for the personal use of the or his/her family 
• Gifts to any person 
• To or on behalf of any other candidate or committee 
• Printers of mass mailings 
• To any person who transfers funds to any other person for expenditures reportable under 
Regulation 18431 
 
 
 
 
4 Regulation 18431(c) also requires a committee to report its subvendor expenditures on its campaign 
statements “at the same time and in the same manner and detail as required under Section 84211 for the […] 
committee’s direct expenditures.”  
 
 
• Any commission, finder’s free or rebate paid to a person in connection with any 
reportable expenditure under Regulation 18431 that is inconsistent in either manner or 
amount with customary industry practice 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(3) 
 
Thus, Regulation 18431 clearly details the types of payments to subvendors—including 
specific products and services—which are reportable under Section 84303 and, thus, also required 
to be itemized on a committee’s campaign statements under Section 84211. 
 
Regulation 18431(a) also provides that “expenditures not of the types included […above] do 
not have to be reported.” (Emphasis added.) 
 
The plain text of Section 84303(a), exempts expenditures for “overhead or normal operating 
expenses” from the subvendor reporting requirements. Therefore, read together with the provision 
in Regulation 18431(a) that “expenditures not of the types included […above] do not have to be 
reported,” a committee is not required to report a subvendor payment if it is made for: i) “overhead 
or normal operating expenses,” which include the kinds of services that the committee’s agent or 
independent contractor is retained to provide; or ii) for a type of subvendor payment not mentioned 
in Regulation 18431. 
 
Interpreting these provisions, we have previously advised that Section 84303 did not require 
disclosure of individual telephone bank workers or of the telephone company providing the rental 
phones to the phone bank, because “these payments are normal operating expenses of a phone 
bank.” (Davidson Advice Letter, No. I-90-107.) More broadly, we advised in the Davidson letter 
that “Candidates and committees are required to report expenditures made on their behalf by agents 
or independent contractors if those expenditures are for other than [sic] the agent’s or independent 
contractor’s normal operating and overhead expenses.” Similarly, in the Trautwein Advice Letter, 
No. I-87-315, we advised that Section 84303 did not require a candidate to report a campaign 
button and bumper sticker manufacturer as a subvendor, so long as the candidate reported the 
candidate’s payments to the campaign management firm who ordered the paraphernalia on behalf of 
the candidate, as well as the subvendor who supplied the campaign management firm with the 
buttons and bumper stickers. 
 
In light of recent informal assistance sought by email, you have made a number of inquiries 
about different types of subvendor payments and whether they need to be itemized by a committee 
in its campaign reports. We answer each one below. 
 
1. Expenses incurred by a campaign consulting firm. Must the following payments made by a 
political committee to a campaign consulting firm as reimbursement for expenses which the 
consulting firm has incurred on behalf of the committee be disclosed as payments to subvendors 
on Schedule G:  
 
 
A. Federal Express or other overnight delivery services to send materials to committee 
representatives. 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(2) provides that products or services related to postage for campaign 
mailings must be reported as subvendor payments under Section 84303. However, internal 
communications between a consulting firm and the committee would not generally be considered a 
campaign mailing. Thus, Federal Express or other overnight delivery services for the 
communications would be considered overhead or normal operating expenses and not reportable 
subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
B. Airlines and hotels for travel to meet with committee representatives or conduct other 
business on behalf of the committee. 
 
No. Travel and lodging costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm in connection with 
regular services provided are not reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303 or 
Regulation 18431. Travel and lodging do not constitute expert advice, analysis, or campaign 
management services within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(1). Nor are travel and lodging 
costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm for travel to conduct its services “products or services 
that show how the campaign is conducted,” within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(2). Thus, 
airfare and hotel costs would be considered “overhead or normal operating” expenses under Section 
84303 and not reportable subvendor payments.5 
 
C. Hourly fees to canvassers, when the firm is retained to conduct grassroots outreach for the 
committee. 
 
“Canvassing” is generally understood to mean “to go through (a district) or go to (persons) in 
order to solicit orders or political support or to determine opinions or sentiments.”6 Regulation 
18431(a)(2)(D) provides that subvendor payments for “surveys, polls, signature gathering and door- 
to-door solicitation of voters” must be reported under Section 84303. (Emphasis added.) While the 
committee must report payments for canvassing, payments to individual canvassers by a campaign 
consulting firm hired by a committee may be reported as payments to the firm for the canvassing 
services and not as separate subvendor payments to the individual canvassers. If reported in this 
manner, names of individual canvassers need not be reported.7 
 
 
5 We note that this conclusion does not apply if the campaign consulting firm is paying the travel and lodging 
costs for a candidate or a member of his/her family. 
 
6 “Canvass.” Merriam-Webster Online Dictionary. 2019. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary (2 Dec. 
2019). 
7 While Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D), requires subvendor payments for “surveys, polls, signature gathering and 
door-to-door solicitation of voters” to be reported in accordance with Sections 84211(k)(6) and 84303(a), we must read 
Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D) in conjunction with applicable case law. The U.S. Supreme Court in Buckley v. American 
Constitutional Law Foundation, 525 U.S. 182 (1999), struck down a Colorado law requiring identification of each paid 
petition circulator and the total amount of money paid to each circulator. In light of Buckley, the Commission itself 
supported emergency legislation to repeal former Section 84211(r) which had required similar disclosure. Accordingly, 
while a subvendor must report any payment to a firm or business entity that is paid to provide signature gathering or 
door-to-door solicitation of voters, the names of individual signature gatherers or door-to-door solicitors need not be 
reported. By extension, the names of individual canvassers also need not be reported.  
 
 
 
D. Hourly fees to petition circulators, when the firm is retained by the committee to circulate an 
initiative petition. 
 
Similar to a payment for canvassers, payments to a campaign consulting firm for individual 
petition circulators hired by the firm may be reported by the committee as payments to the firm for 
its services and not as separate subvendor payments requiring the names of individual signature 
gatherers. 
 
(See 1C., above.) 
 
E. Fees to another campaign consulting firm to help the firm develop strategic advice, when the 
firm is retained to provide strategic advice to the committee. 
 
Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) specifically provides that subvendor payments for advice 
concerning campaign strategy must be reported. 
 
F. Fees to outside graphic designers retained because the firm's in-house graphic designers are 
at full capacity during the busy campaign season, when the firm is retained to create graphics, 
logos, artwork, etc. for the committee. 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor payments for products or services related to 
campaign literature, campaign buttons and other paraphernalia, to be reported. Accordingly, 
subvendor payments to on outside graphic designer for campaign materials must be reported. 
 
2. Expenses and fees paid by a law firm. Do the following payments made by a political 
committee to a law firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor payments? 
 
A. Westlaw, Lexis, or other legal research services. 
 
No. Legal research is “the kind of service” that a law firm retained to provide legal counsel 
is hired to provide. These types of legal research services are customarily part of overhead or 
normal operating expenses for a law firm. Such services need not be reported as subvendor 
payments under Section 84303. 
 
B. Filing fees to a court in connection with a lawsuit involving the committee. 
 
No. Court filing fees are normal operating expenses and need not be reported 
as subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
C. Amounts paid to the state or local government to settle enforcement matters. 
 
Yes. Payments to state and local governments for settlement amounts reached in 
enforcement matters on behalf of clients must be reported by the committees directly and not as 
payments by subvendors. These are not payments that fall within any exception from reporting. 
They are not overhead or normal operating expenses of a subcontractor and they are not payments 
for services. They are debts owed by a client that are passed through the law firm. If a law firm  
 
 
sends payment to a government agency for a settlement on behalf of a client, the committee should 
note this on its campaign reports when reporting the expenditure. 
 
D. Expert witness fees in a lawsuit involving the committee. 
 
No. See 2B above. 
 
E. Fees paid to independent contractor attorneys to help the law firm research and prepare 
pleadings for a lawsuit being pursued by the committee. 
 
No. See 2A, above.8 
 
3. Expenses and fees paid by a political treasurer firm. Do payments made by a political 
committee to a political treasurer firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor 
payments? 
 
A. Banking fees. 
 
No. Under the Act, it is a campaign treasurer’s duty to ensure that the committee complies 
with all requirements concerning receipts, expenditures, and reporting and recordkeeping. 
(Sections 81004, 84100, 84213, and Regulation 18427). In carrying out these duties, a treasurer is 
generally responsible for staying informed of bank deposits and proper expenditures, including 
ensuring that the committee complies with the so-called “one bank account rule.” (Section 85201 
and Regulation 18521.) 
 
Thus, a treasurer’s duties often include management of the committee’s bank account and it is 
within the realm of the “kind of service” a political treasurer firm offers for the firm to cover a 
committees banking fees. 
 
B. Late filing fees paid to the Secretary of State or local filing officer. 
 
Yes. See 2C, above. 
 
C. Campaign reporting software licensing fees (e.g., Netfile, NGP, etc.). 
 
No. These are normal operating expenses of campaign treasurers. 
 
D. Fees to credit card processing firms for processing contributions (Democracy Engine, 
ActBlue, etc.) 
 
Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) provides that analysis, advice, or management services in 
connection with, among other things, campaign fundraising must be reported as a subvendor 
payment. Additionally, Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor reporting for products or 
services that show how a campaign is conducted. While making payments to a credit card 
 
8 We note that this conclusion is limited to a law firm hired exclusively to research and prepare pleadings for a 
lawsuit. Any other legal services provided may be reportable depending on the nature of the services.  
 
 
processing firm alone does not constitute analysis or advice, a campaign treasurer’s payment of 
a committee/candidate’s ActBlue or Democracy Engine fees does constitute management 
services in connection with campaign fundraising and also shows how a campaign is being 
conducted. As such, these payments are reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
E. Contract data entry help during busy reporting seasons. 
 
No. Data entry alone would fall under overhead or normal operating expenses that are kind 
of services that the committee’s agent or independent contractor is retained to provide.
sections.facts:
Your law firm represents numerous campaign committees that have reporting obligations 
under the Act. 
You seek advice as to whether certain types of payments made by campaign consulting 
firms, law firms, and political treasurer firms retained by a committee must be disclosed as 
payments to subvendors on Schedule G of the committee’s Form 460.3

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: S AND CONCLUSIONS

statements. For expenditures of $100 or more made to a single payee, committees must provide the 
name and address of the payee as well as the amount and a description of the payment. (Section2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
3 This letter supersedes the Sutton Advice Letter, No. I-19-190, and is issued in reconsideration of the issues 
presented.  
 
 
84211(k).) For payments made through an independent contractor or agent, commonly referred to 
as subvendor payments, Section 84211(k)(6) provides that committees must disclose this same 
information for “each person, if different than the payee, who has provided consideration for an 
expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more during the period covered by the campaign 
statement.” (Section 84211(k)(6).) 
 
In conjunction with Section 84211(k)(6), Section 84303(a) provides that a committee must 
report any expenditure of $500 or more made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the 
committee as if the committee had made the expenditure itself, unless the expenditure is for 
“overhead or normal operating expenses.” Regulation 18431 also sets forth what types of 
expenditures by a committee’s agent or independent contractor must be reported under Section 
84303, including payments to vendors and subvendors.4 Under Regulation 18431, three categories 
of payments to a subvendor are reportable: 
 
1) Analysis, advice, or management services in connection with: 
• Development of campaign strategy 
• Campaign management 
• Design or management of campaign literature or advertising 
• Campaign fundraising 
Regulation 18431(a)(1). 
2) Products or services that show how the campaign is conducted including but not limited to: 
• Printed campaign literature 
• Advertising time or space 
• Campaign buttons and other campaign paraphernalia 
• Surveys, polls, signature gathering, and door-to-door solicitation of voters 
• Facilities, invitations or entertainment for fundraising events 
• Postage for campaign mailings 
Regulation 18431(a)(2). 
3) Any of the following expenditures: 
• To the candidate 
• To any person for the personal use of the or his/her family 
• Gifts to any person 
• To or on behalf of any other candidate or committee 
• Printers of mass mailings 
• To any person who transfers funds to any other person for expenditures reportable under 
Regulation 18431 
 
 
 
 
4 Regulation 18431(c) also requires a committee to report its subvendor expenditures on its campaign 
statements “at the same time and in the same manner and detail as required under Section 84211 for the […] 
committee’s direct expenditures.”  
 
 
• Any commission, finder’s free or rebate paid to a person in connection with any 
reportable expenditure under Regulation 18431 that is inconsistent in either manner or 
amount with customary industry practice 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(3) 
 
Thus, Regulation 18431 clearly details the types of payments to subvendors—including 
specific products and services—which are reportable under Section 84303 and, thus, also required 
to be itemized on a committee’s campaign statements under Section 84211. 
 
Regulation 18431(a) also provides that “expenditures not of the types included […above] do 
not have to be reported.” (Emphasis added.) 
 
The plain text of Section 84303(a), exempts expenditures for “overhead or normal operating 
expenses” from the subvendor reporting requirements. Therefore, read together with the provision 
in Regulation 18431(a) that “expenditures not of the types included […above] do not have to be 
reported,” a committee is not required to report a subvendor payment if it is made for: i) “overhead 
or normal operating expenses,” which include the kinds of services that the committee’s agent or 
independent contractor is retained to provide; or ii) for a type of subvendor payment not mentioned 
in Regulation 18431. 
 
Interpreting these provisions, we have previously advised that Section 84303 did not require 
disclosure of individual telephone bank workers or of the telephone company providing the rental 
phones to the phone bank, because “these payments are normal operating expenses of a phone 
bank.” (Davidson Advice Letter, No. I-90-107.) More broadly, we advised in the Davidson letter 
that “Candidates and committees are required to report expenditures made on their behalf by agents 
or independent contractors if those expenditures are for other than [sic] the agent’s or independent 
contractor’s normal operating and overhead expenses.” Similarly, in the Trautwein Advice Letter, 
No. I-87-315, we advised that Section 84303 did not require a candidate to report a campaign 
button and bumper sticker manufacturer as a subvendor, so long as the candidate reported the 
candidate’s payments to the campaign management firm who ordered the paraphernalia on behalf of 
the candidate, as well as the subvendor who supplied the campaign management firm with the 
buttons and bumper stickers. 
 
In light of recent informal assistance sought by email, you have made a number of inquiries 
about different types of subvendor payments and whether they need to be itemized by a committee 
in its campaign reports. We answer each one below. 
 
1. Expenses incurred by a campaign consulting firm. Must the following payments made by a 
political committee to a campaign consulting firm as reimbursement for expenses which the 
consulting firm has incurred on behalf of the committee be disclosed as payments to subvendors 
on Schedule G:  
 
 
A. Federal Express or other overnight delivery services to send materials to committee 
representatives. 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(2) provides that products or services related to postage for campaign 
mailings must be reported as subvendor payments under Section 84303. However, internal 
communications between a consulting firm and the committee would not generally be considered a 
campaign mailing. Thus, Federal Express or other overnight delivery services for the 
communications would be considered overhead or normal operating expenses and not reportable 
subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
B. Airlines and hotels for travel to meet with committee representatives or conduct other 
business on behalf of the committee. 
 
No. Travel and lodging costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm in connection with 
regular services provided are not reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303 or 
Regulation 18431. Travel and lodging do not constitute expert advice, analysis, or campaign 
management services within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(1). Nor are travel and lodging 
costs incurred by a campaign consulting firm for travel to conduct its services “products or services 
that show how the campaign is conducted,” within the meaning of Regulation 18431(a)(2). Thus, 
airfare and hotel costs would be considered “overhead or normal operating” expenses under Section 
84303 and not reportable subvendor payments.5 
 
C. Hourly fees to canvassers, when the firm is retained to conduct grassroots outreach for the 
committee. 
 
“Canvassing” is generally understood to mean “to go through (a district) or go to (persons) in 
order to solicit orders or political support or to determine opinions or sentiments.”6 Regulation 
18431(a)(2)(D) provides that subvendor payments for “surveys, polls, signature gathering and door- 
to-door solicitation of voters” must be reported under Section 84303. (Emphasis added.) While the 
committee must report payments for canvassing, payments to individual canvassers by a campaign 
consulting firm hired by a committee may be reported as payments to the firm for the canvassing 
services and not as separate subvendor payments to the individual canvassers. If reported in this 
manner, names of individual canvassers need not be reported.7 
 
 
5 We note that this conclusion does not apply if the campaign consulting firm is paying the travel and lodging 
costs for a candidate or a member of his/her family. 
 
6 “Canvass.” Merriam-Webster Online Dictionary. 2019. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary (2 Dec. 
2019). 
7 While Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D), requires subvendor payments for “surveys, polls, signature gathering and 
door-to-door solicitation of voters” to be reported in accordance with Sections 84211(k)(6) and 84303(a), we must read 
Regulation 18431(a)(2)(D) in conjunction with applicable case law. The U.S. Supreme Court in Buckley v. American 
Constitutional Law Foundation, 525 U.S. 182 (1999), struck down a Colorado law requiring identification of each paid 
petition circulator and the total amount of money paid to each circulator. In light of Buckley, the Commission itself 
supported emergency legislation to repeal former Section 84211(r) which had required similar disclosure. Accordingly, 
while a subvendor must report any payment to a firm or business entity that is paid to provide signature gathering or 
door-to-door solicitation of voters, the names of individual signature gatherers or door-to-door solicitors need not be 
reported. By extension, the names of individual canvassers also need not be reported.  
 
 
 
D. Hourly fees to petition circulators, when the firm is retained by the committee to circulate an 
initiative petition. 
 
Similar to a payment for canvassers, payments to a campaign consulting firm for individual 
petition circulators hired by the firm may be reported by the committee as payments to the firm for 
its services and not as separate subvendor payments requiring the names of individual signature 
gatherers. 
 
(See 1C., above.) 
 
E. Fees to another campaign consulting firm to help the firm develop strategic advice, when the 
firm is retained to provide strategic advice to the committee. 
 
Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) specifically provides that subvendor payments for advice 
concerning campaign strategy must be reported. 
 
F. Fees to outside graphic designers retained because the firm's in-house graphic designers are 
at full capacity during the busy campaign season, when the firm is retained to create graphics, 
logos, artwork, etc. for the committee. 
 
Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor payments for products or services related to 
campaign literature, campaign buttons and other paraphernalia, to be reported. Accordingly, 
subvendor payments to on outside graphic designer for campaign materials must be reported. 
 
2. Expenses and fees paid by a law firm. Do the following payments made by a political 
committee to a law firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor payments? 
 
A. Westlaw, Lexis, or other legal research services. 
 
No. Legal research is “the kind of service” that a law firm retained to provide legal counsel 
is hired to provide. These types of legal research services are customarily part of overhead or 
normal operating expenses for a law firm. Such services need not be reported as subvendor 
payments under Section 84303. 
 
B. Filing fees to a court in connection with a lawsuit involving the committee. 
 
No. Court filing fees are normal operating expenses and need not be reported 
as subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
C. Amounts paid to the state or local government to settle enforcement matters. 
 
Yes. Payments to state and local governments for settlement amounts reached in 
enforcement matters on behalf of clients must be reported by the committees directly and not as 
payments by subvendors. These are not payments that fall within any exception from reporting. 
They are not overhead or normal operating expenses of a subcontractor and they are not payments 
for services. They are debts owed by a client that are passed through the law firm. If a law firm  
 
 
sends payment to a government agency for a settlement on behalf of a client, the committee should 
note this on its campaign reports when reporting the expenditure. 
 
D. Expert witness fees in a lawsuit involving the committee. 
 
No. See 2B above. 
 
E. Fees paid to independent contractor attorneys to help the law firm research and prepare 
pleadings for a lawsuit being pursued by the committee. 
 
No. See 2A, above.8 
 
3. Expenses and fees paid by a political treasurer firm. Do payments made by a political 
committee to a political treasurer firm for the following purposes count as reportable subvendor 
payments? 
 
A. Banking fees. 
 
No. Under the Act, it is a campaign treasurer’s duty to ensure that the committee complies 
with all requirements concerning receipts, expenditures, and reporting and recordkeeping. 
(Sections 81004, 84100, 84213, and Regulation 18427). In carrying out these duties, a treasurer is 
generally responsible for staying informed of bank deposits and proper expenditures, including 
ensuring that the committee complies with the so-called “one bank account rule.” (Section 85201 
and Regulation 18521.) 
 
Thus, a treasurer’s duties often include management of the committee’s bank account and it is 
within the realm of the “kind of service” a political treasurer firm offers for the firm to cover a 
committees banking fees. 
 
B. Late filing fees paid to the Secretary of State or local filing officer. 
 
Yes. See 2C, above. 
 
C. Campaign reporting software licensing fees (e.g., Netfile, NGP, etc.). 
 
No. These are normal operating expenses of campaign treasurers. 
 
D. Fees to credit card processing firms for processing contributions (Democracy Engine, 
ActBlue, etc.) 
 
Yes. Regulation 18431(a)(1) provides that analysis, advice, or management services in 
connection with, among other things, campaign fundraising must be reported as a subvendor 
payment. Additionally, Regulation 18431(a)(2) requires subvendor reporting for products or 
services that show how a campaign is conducted. While making payments to a credit card 
 
8 We note that this conclusion is limited to a law firm hired exclusively to research and prepare pleadings for a 
lawsuit. Any other legal services provided may be reportable depending on the nature of the services.  
 
 
processing firm alone does not constitute analysis or advice, a campaign treasurer’s payment of 
a committee/candidate’s ActBlue or Democracy Engine fees does constitute management 
services in connection with campaign fundraising and also shows how a campaign is being 
conducted. As such, these payments are reportable subvendor payments under Section 84303. 
 
E. Contract data entry help during busy reporting seasons. 
 
No. Data entry alone would fall under overhead or normal operating expenses that are kind 
of services that the committee’s agent or independent contractor is retained to provide.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 30, 2020 Sutton Advice Letter No. I-19-190(a) is SUPERSEDED by Sutton Advice Letter No. I-19-190 James R. Sutton 150 Post Street, Suite 405 San Francisco, CA 94108 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-19-190(a) Dear Mr. Sutton: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes the facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER Your law firm represents numerous campaign committees that have reporting obligations under the Act. You seek advice as to whether certain types of payments made by campaign consulting firms, law firms, and political treasurer firms retained by a committee on Schedule G of the committee's Form 460.³ QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS Section 84211 of the Act specifies what information must be disclosed on campaign statements. For expenditures of $100 or more made to a single payee, committees must provide the name and address of the payee as well as the amount and a description of the payment. (Section ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) ³ This letter supersedes the Sutton Advice Letter, No. I-19-190, and is issued in reconsideration of the issues presented. File No. 1-19-190 Page No. 2 84211(k).). For payments made through an independent contractor or agent, commonly referred to as subvendor payments, Section 84211(k)(6) provides that committees must disclose this same information for "each person, if different than the payee, who has provided consideration for an expenditure of five hundred dollars ($500) or more during the period covered by the campaign statement." (Section 84211(k)(6).) In conjunction with Section 84211(k)(6), Section 84303(a) provides that a committee must report any expenditure of $500 or more made by an agent or independent contractor on behalf of the committee as if the committee had made the expenditure itself, unless the expenditure is for "overhead or normal operating expenses." Regulation 18431 also sets forth what types of expenditures by a committee's agent or independent contractor must be reported under Section 84303,
